concepts.name: adaboost.m1
concepts.description: A boosting algorithm that sequentially applies a weak classifier to reweighted data, upweighting misclassified observations, and combines classifiers via weighted majority vote.

concepts.name: forward stagewise additive modeling
concepts.description: An approach to fitting additive models f(x) = Σ βm b(x;γm) by sequentially adding basis functions without modifying previously added terms.

concepts.name: exponential loss for classification
concepts.description: Loss function L(y, f(x)) = exp(-y f(x)) used by AdaBoost, where y ∈ {-1,1}. Leads to simple reweighting algorithm but sensitive to outliers.

concepts.name: binomial deviance
concepts.description: Loss function -[y' log p(x) + (1-y') log(1-p(x))] for binary classification, where p(x) = e^f(x)/(1+e^f(x)). More robust than exponential loss to outliers and mislabeling.

concepts.name: huber loss
concepts.description: A robust regression loss criterion that is quadratic for small residuals |y-f(x)| ≤ δ and linear for large residuals, providing resistance to outliers.

concepts.name: gradient boosted models
concepts.description: A generalization of tree boosting that fits additive models using any differentiable loss function, providing robustness and accuracy for data mining.

concepts.name: partial dependence plot
concepts.description: A visualization showing the marginal effect of selected predictors on the predicted outcome, averaging over the other predictors to reveal predictor contributions.

concepts.name: steepest descent
concepts.description: An optimization method that chooses the increment vector as negative of the gradient times a scalar step size, moving in the direction of most rapid decrease.

concepts.name: gradient boosting
concepts.description: A boosting technique that uses the gradient of the loss function to fit trees at each iteration, generalizing boosting to arbitrary differentiable loss functions.

concepts.name: right-sized trees for boosting
concepts.description: The practice of using trees of fixed size J in boosting, where J controls the level of interaction effects that can be captured.

concepts.name: anova expansion
concepts.description: A decomposition of a function into main effects, second-order interactions, and higher-order interactions between variables.

concepts.name: learning rate in boosting
concepts.description: The parameter ν controlling how much each tree contributes to the boosted model, with smaller values requiring more iterations but often yielding better test error.

concepts.name: subsampling in boosting
concepts.description: Sampling a fraction of training data at each boosting iteration to reduce computation time and often improve accuracy.

concepts.name: relative importance of predictor variables
concepts.description: A measure quantifying the relevance of each input variable in boosted tree models, based on squared improvements in the splitting criterion.

concepts.name: weak learner
concepts.description: An algorithm for producing a two-class classifier with performance guaranteed to be significantly better than random guessing.

concepts.name: single index model in econometrics
concepts.description:
2)(1, 1)T ,
so that the function only varies in the direction X1 + X2. In the example
on the right, ω = (1, 0).
The PPR model (11.1) is very general, since the operation of forming
nonlinear functions of linear combinations generates a surprisingly large
class of models. For example, the product X1 ·X2 can be written as [(X1 +
X2)2 −(X1 −X2)2]/4, and higher-order products can be represented simi-
larly.
In fact, if M is taken arbitrarily large, for appropriate choice of gm the
PPR model can approximate any continuous function in IRp arbitrarily
well. Such a class of models is called a universal approximator. However
this generality comes at a price. Interpretation of the ﬁtted model is usually
diﬃcult, because each input enters into the model in a complex and multi-
faceted way. As a result, the PPR model is most useful for prediction, and
not very useful for producing an understandable model for the data. The
M = 1 model, known as the single index model in econometrics, is an
exception. It is slightly more general than the linear regression model, and
oﬀers a similar interpretation.
How do we ﬁt a PPR model, given training data (xi, yi), i = 1, 2, . . . , N?
We seek the approximate minimizers of the error function

concepts.name: back-propagation equations
concepts.description:
known as the back-propagation equations. Using this, the updates in (11.13)
can be implemented with a two-pass algorithm. In the forward pass, the
current weights are ﬁxed and the predicted values ˆfk(xi) are computed
from formula (11.5). In the backward pass, the errors δki are computed,
and then back-propagated via (11.15) to give the errors smi. Both sets of
errors are then used to compute the gradients for the updates in (11.13),
via (11.14).

concepts.name: back-propagation
concepts.description:
This two-pass procedure is what is known as back-propagation. It has
also been called the delta rule (Widrow and Hoﬀ, 1960). The computational
components for cross-entropy have the same form as those for the sum of
squares error function, and are derived in Exercise 11.3.
The advantages of back-propagation are its simple, local nature. In the
back propagation algorithm, each hidden unit passes and receives infor-
mation only to and from units that share a connection. Hence it can be
implemented eﬃciently on a parallel architecture computer.
The updates in (11.13) are a kind of batch learning, with the parame-
ter updates being a sum over all of the training cases. Learning can also
be carried out online—processing each observation one at a time, updat-
ing the gradient after each training case, and cycling through the training
cases many times. In this case, the sums in equations (11.13) are replaced
by a single summand. A training epoch refers to one sweep through the
entire training set. Online training allows the network to handle very large
training sets, and also to update the weights as new observations come in.
The learning rate γr for batch learning is usually taken to be a con-
stant, and can also be optimized by a line search that minimizes the error
function at each update. With online learning γr should decrease to zero
as the iteration r →∞. This learning is a form of stochastic approxima-
tion (Robbins and Munro, 1951); results in this ﬁeld ensure convergence if
γr →0, 	
r γr = ∞, and 	
r γ2
r < ∞(satisﬁed, for example, by γr = 1/r).
Back-propagation can be very slow, and for that reason is usually not
the method of choice. Second-order techniques such as Newton’s method
are not attractive here, because the second derivative matrix of R (the
Hessian) can be very large. Better approaches to ﬁtting include conjugate
gradients and variable metric methods. These avoid explicit computation
of the second derivative matrix while still providing faster convergence.

concepts.name: weight elimination penalty
concepts.description:
known as the weight elimination penalty. This has the eﬀect of shrinking
smaller weights more than (11.16) does.
Figure 11.4 shows the result of training a neural network with ten hidden
units, without weight decay (upper panel) and with weight decay (lower
panel), to the mixture example of Chapter 2. Weight decay has clearly
improved the prediction. Figure 11.5 shows heat maps of the estimated
weights from the training (grayscale versions of these are called Hinton
diagrams.) We see that weight decay has dampened the weights in both
layers: the resulting weights are spread fairly evenly over the ten hidden
units.

concepts.name: convolutional networks
concepts.description:
reduces considerably the total number of weights. With many more hidden
units than Net-2, Net-3 has fewer links and hence weights (1226 vs. 3214),
and achieves similar performance.
Net-4 and Net-5 have local connectivity with shared weights. All units
in a local feature map perform the same operation on diﬀerent parts of the
image, achieved by sharing the same weights. The ﬁrst hidden layer of Net-
4 has two 8×8 arrays, and each unit takes input from a 3×3 patch just like
in Net-3. However, each of the units in a single 8×8 feature map share the
same set of nine weights (but have their own bias parameter). This forces
the extracted features in diﬀerent parts of the image to be computed by
the same linear functional, and consequently these networks are sometimes
known as convolutional networks. The second hidden layer of Net-4 has
no weight sharing, and is the same as in Net-3. The gradient of the error
function R with respect to a shared weight is the sum of the gradients of
R with respect to each connection controlled by the weights in question.
Table 11.1 gives the number of links, the number of weights and the
optimal test performance for each of the networks. We see that Net-4 has
more links but fewer weights than Net-3, and superior test performance.
Net-5 has four 4 × 4 feature maps in the second hidden layer, each unit
connected to a 5 × 5 local patch in the layer below. Weights are shared
in each of these feature maps. We see that Net-5 does the best, having
errors of only 1.6%, compared to 13% for the “vanilla” network Net-2.
The clever design of network Net-5, motivated by the fact that features of
handwriting style should appear in more than one part of a digit, was the
result of many person years of experimentation. This and similar networks
gave better performance on ZIP code problems than any other learning
method at that time (early 1990s). This example also shows that neural
networks are not a fully automatic tool, as they are sometimes advertised.
As with all statistical models, subject matter knowledge can and should be
used to improve their performance.
This network was later outperformed by the tangent distance approach
(Simard et al., 1993) described in Section 13.3.3, which explicitly incorpo-
rates natural aﬃne invariances. At this point the digit recognition datasets
become test beds for every new learning procedure, and researchers worked

concepts.name: single hidden layer neural network
concepts.description: Neural network with one layer of hidden units between inputs and outputs.

concepts.name: weight decay
concepts.description: A regularization method for neural networks analogous to ridge regression, adding an L2 penalty on the weights to the error function.

concepts.name: universal approximator
concepts.description: A class of models that can approximate any continuous function arbitrarily well (e.g., projection pursuit regression with enough terms).

concepts.name: pseudo residuals
concepts.description: The negative gradient components in gradient boosting, also called generalized residuals, which serve as targets for fitting regression trees.

concepts.name: main effect
concepts.description: The contribution of a single predictor variable to the target function, independent of interactions with other variables.

concepts.name: decision stump
concepts.description: A tree with a single split (J=2), producing boosted models with only main effects and no interactions.

concepts.name: early stopping
concepts.description: A regularization strategy where training is terminated before reaching the global minimum, monitoring validation error to prevent overfitting.

concepts.name: shrinkage in boosting
concepts.description: Scaling the contribution of each tree by a learning rate factor 0 < ν < 1 when added to the current approximation, controlling the learning rate of boosting.

concepts.name: partial dependence plots
concepts.description: Graphical visualization showing the marginal effect of a subset of predictor variables on the prediction function after accounting for the average effects of other variables.

concepts.name: projection pursuit regression
concepts.description: A regression model that fits additive nonlinear functions of linear combinations (projections) of the inputs, where both the projection directions and ridge functions are estimated from data.

concepts.name: ridge function
concepts.description: A function in p-dimensional space that varies only in the direction defined by a vector, of the form g(ωᵀX).

concepts.name: single index model
concepts.description: A projection pursuit regression model with M=1, known in econometrics, slightly more general than linear regression.

concepts.name: gauss-newton search
concepts.description: A quasi-Newton optimization method that discards the part of the Hessian involving second derivatives, used for fitting projection pursuit models.

concepts.name: single hidden layer perceptron
concepts.description: A neural network with one hidden layer, also called a vanilla neural net or single hidden layer back-propagation network.

concepts.name: sigmoid activation function
concepts.description: The nonlinear function σ(v) = 1/(1 + e⁻ᵛ) used to introduce nonlinearity in neural networks.

concepts.name: radial basis function network
concepts.description: A neural network variant that uses Gaussian radial basis functions for the activation function σ(v).

concepts.name: softmax activation function
concepts.description: An output transformation for K-class classification that produces positive probability estimates summing to one, identical to the multilogit model.

concepts.name: hidden units
concepts.description: The middle layer units in a neural network that compute derived features Zm, whose values are not directly observed.

concepts.name: delta rule
concepts.description: Another name for the back-propagation algorithm for training neural networks.

concepts.name: batch learning
concepts.description: A training approach where parameter updates are computed as a sum over all training cases before updating.

concepts.name: online learning
concepts.description: A training approach that processes one observation at a time, updating gradients after each training case and cycling through the training set multiple times.

concepts.name: training epoch
concepts.description: One complete sweep through the entire training set in online learning.

concepts.name: stochastic approximation
concepts.description: A class of iterative methods for optimization where the learning rate decreases to zero as iterations progress, ensuring convergence.

concepts.name: conjugate gradients
concepts.description: An optimization method that avoids explicit computation of the Hessian while providing faster convergence than back-propagation.

concepts.name: variable metric methods
concepts.description: Optimization techniques that avoid explicit Hessian computation while achieving faster convergence than gradient descent.

concepts.name: hinton diagrams
concepts.description: Grayscale heat maps showing estimated neural network weights, used to visualize and interpret what features each hidden unit extracts.

concepts.name: local connectivity
concepts.description: A neural network constraint where each hidden unit connects only to a small patch of units in the layer below, reducing total parameters.

concepts.name: weight sharing
concepts.description: A constraint where multiple units in a feature map use the same set of weights, forcing extracted features to be computed by the same linear functional across different image regions.

concepts.name: feature maps
concepts.description: Arrays of hidden units in convolutional networks that share weights and extract the same type of feature across different regions of the input.

concepts.name: receptive field
concepts.description: The region of input that a hidden unit receives connections from in a locally connected network.

concepts.name: hybrid monte carlo
concepts.description: An MCMC approach that includes an auxiliary momentum vector and implements Hamiltonian dynamics to avoid random walk behavior and achieve faster convergence.

concepts.name: automatic relevance determination
concepts.description: A Bayesian feature selection method where weights for each feature share a common prior variance, and features with small posterior variances are discarded.

concepts.name: boosted neural networks
concepts.description: Applying boosting to neural networks, typically using weak learners (small networks with few hidden units) as base learners.

concepts.name: screened features
concepts.description: Features that have been filtered or selected before model training, creating a legitimate self-contained procedure.

concepts.name: ard reduced features
concepts.description: Features reduced using Automatic Relevance Determination from Bayesian neural networks, representing a data-driven dimensionality reduction.

concepts.name: neural network computational complexity
concepts.description: The computational cost of fitting neural networks, typically requiring O(NpML) operations where N is observations, p is predictors, M is hidden units, and L is training epochs.

concepts.name: tangent distance
concepts.description: An invariant distance metric for nearest-neighbor classification that explicitly incorporates natural affine invariances.

concepts.name: support vectors
concepts.description:
with nonzero coeﬃcients ˆαi only for those observations i for which the
constraints in (12.16) are exactly met (due to (12.14)). These observations
are called the support vectors, since ˆβ is represented in terms of them
alone. Among these support points, some will lie on the edge of the margin
(ˆξi = 0), and hence from (12.15) and (12.12) will be characterized by
0 < ˆαi < C; the remainder (ˆξi > 0) have ˆαi = C. From (12.14) we can
see that any of these margin points (0 < ˆαi, ˆξi = 0) can be used to solve
for β0, and we typically use an average of all the solutions for numerical
stability.
Maximizing the dual (12.13) is a simpler convex quadratic programming
problem than the primal (12.9), and can be solved with standard techniques
(Murray et al., 1981, for example).
Given the solutions ˆβ0 and ˆβ, the decision function can be written as

concepts.name: FDA
concepts.description:
In the remainder of this chapter we describe a class of techniques that
attend to all these issues by generalizing the LDA model. This is achieved
largely by three diﬀerent ideas.
The ﬁrst idea is to recast the LDA problem as a linear regression problem.
Many techniques exist for generalizing linear regression to more ﬂexible,
nonparametric forms of regression. This in turn leads to more ﬂexible forms
of discriminant analysis, which we call FDA. In most cases of interest, the

concepts.name: penalized discriminant analysis
concepts.description:
The choice of Ω depends on the problem. If ηℓ(x) = h(x)βℓis an expansion
on spline basis functions, Ω might constrain ηℓto be smooth over IRp. In
the case of additive splines, there are N spline basis functions for each
coordinate, resulting in a total of Np basis functions in h(x); Ω in this case
is Np × Np and block diagonal.
The steps in FDA can then be viewed as a generalized form of LDA,
which we call penalized discriminant analysis, or PDA:

concepts.name: kernel function
concepts.description: A function K(x, x') that computes inner products in a transformed feature space without explicitly computing the transformation h(x).

concepts.name: optimal separating hyperplane
concepts.description: The hyperplane that separates two classes while maximizing the margin (minimum distance) to the closest point from either class, providing a unique solution.

concepts.name: wolfe dual
concepts.description: The dual formulation of a constrained optimization problem, obtained by substituting optimality conditions into the Lagrangian, often simpler to solve.

concepts.name: karush-kuhn-tucker conditions
concepts.description: Necessary and sufficient conditions for optimality in constrained optimization, including the primal-dual relationships and complementarity slackness.

concepts.name: basis expansion
concepts.description: A technique of augmenting or replacing input variables X with transformations hm(X) to create a richer feature space, then fitting linear models in this expanded space.

concepts.name: bayesian neural networks
concepts.description: Neural networks trained using Bayesian inference with MCMC methods to sample from the posterior distribution of parameters and average predictions.

concepts.name: bagged neural networks
concepts.description: Applying bootstrap aggregating (bagging) to neural networks by training multiple networks on bootstrap resamples and averaging predictions.

concepts.name: support vector classifier
concepts.description: An extension of optimal separating hyperplanes to nonseparable cases where classes overlap, allowing some points on the wrong side of the margin using slack variables.

concepts.name: margin
concepts.description: The distance M from the separating hyperplane to the closest training point, where M = 1/||β||.

concepts.name: slack variables
concepts.description: Variables ξi that allow points to violate the margin constraints in support vector classifiers, measuring the degree of overlap between classes.

concepts.name: convex optimization
concepts.description: An optimization problem with quadratic criterion and linear inequality constraints that has a unique global solution.

concepts.name: nonconvex optimization
concepts.description: An optimization problem that may have multiple local minima, resulting from certain formulations like measuring overlap in actual distance from margin.

concepts.name: kernel methods
concepts.description: Techniques that produce nonlinear decision boundaries by constructing linear boundaries in transformed high-dimensional feature spaces.

concepts.name: svm for classification
concepts.description: Support vector machine adapted for classification tasks by finding optimal decision boundaries in feature space.

concepts.name: linear decision boundary
concepts.description: A hyperplane that separates classes in the original feature space, defined by f(x) = x^T β + β0 = 0.

concepts.name: nonlinear decision boundary
concepts.description: A decision boundary that is not a hyperplane, achieved by SVMs through kernel transformations of the feature space.

concepts.name: feature space transformation
concepts.description: Mapping input features to a higher-dimensional space where linear boundaries can represent complex nonlinear patterns.

concepts.name: signed distance
concepts.description: The distance from a point x to a hyperplane, with sign indicating which side of the boundary, given by f(x) = x^T β + β0.

concepts.name: margin constraint
concepts.description: Constraints ensuring that all training points are at least distance M from the decision boundary: yi*f(xi) >= M for all i.

concepts.name: overlap penalty
concepts.description: A penalty term Σξi that measures the total amount of overlap when classes are not perfectly separable.

concepts.name: cost parameter
concepts.description: A tuning parameter C that controls the tradeoff between margin width and training error in support vector classifiers.

concepts.name: polynomial kernel
concepts.description: A kernel function of the form K(x, x') = (1 + ⟨x, x'⟩)^d that computes polynomial feature expansions efficiently.

concepts.name: radial basis kernel
concepts.description: A kernel function K(x, x') = exp(-γ||x - x'||²) that measures similarity based on Euclidean distance, also known as RBF or Gaussian kernel.

concepts.name: neural network kernel
concepts.description: A kernel function K(x, x') = tanh(κ1⟨x, x'⟩ + κ2) inspired by neural network activation functions.

concepts.name: lagrange multipliers
concepts.description: Optimization variables (αi) used to solve constrained optimization problems by converting them to dual form.

concepts.name: lagrangian primal function
concepts.description: The original formulation of the optimization problem before conversion to dual form, incorporating constraints via Lagrange multipliers.

concepts.name: inner product representation
concepts.description: A formulation where the optimization problem and solution depend on input features only through inner products, enabling the kernel trick.

concepts.name: hinge loss
concepts.description: A loss function L(y, f) = [1 - yf]+ used in SVMs that penalizes points within the margin or on the wrong side.

concepts.name: loss plus penalty formulation
concepts.description: The reformulation of SVMs as minimizing a loss function (hinge loss) plus a penalty term (regularization), connecting SVMs to other statistical methods.

concepts.name: regularization parameter
concepts.description: Parameter λ (equal to 1/C) that controls the tradeoff between model complexity and training error in the loss+penalty formulation.

concepts.name: linearly separable
concepts.description: A property of two classes when there exists a hyperplane that perfectly separates them with no overlap.

concepts.name: nonseparable case
concepts.description: A situation where two classes overlap in feature space and cannot be perfectly separated by a hyperplane.

concepts.name: svm path algorithm
concepts.description: An efficient algorithm for fitting the entire sequence of SVM models obtained by varying the cost parameter C, similar to LARS for lasso.

concepts.name: multiclass svm
concepts.description: Extension of binary SVM to multiclass problems by building classifiers for each pair of classes and using voting or multinomial loss.

concepts.name: one-versus-one classification
concepts.description: A multiclass strategy where a binary classifier is built for each pair of classes, with the final prediction based on voting.

concepts.name: overfit boundary
concepts.description: An excessively complex, wiggly decision boundary that fits training data too closely, resulting from large values of the cost parameter C.

concepts.name: smooth boundary
concepts.description: A decision boundary with low complexity achieved by small values of ||β||, controlled by a small cost parameter C.

concepts.name: tuning parameter
concepts.description: A parameter (such as C in SVMs) that controls model complexity and must be selected via methods like cross-validation.

concepts.name: epsilon-insensitive loss
concepts.description: A loss function for SVM regression that ignores errors smaller than ε, analogous to how SVMs ignore well-classified points far from the boundary.

concepts.name: svm for regression
concepts.description: Extension of support vector machines to regression problems using epsilon-insensitive loss to create a tube around the prediction function.

concepts.name: smoothing splines
concepts.description: A regression method using spline basis functions with penalty on roughness, related to projection pursuit and regularization methods.

concepts.name: radial basis function expansion
concepts.description: A basis expansion method using radial basis functions, mathematically equivalent to certain kernel methods and regularization estimates.

concepts.name: regularization estimate
concepts.description: A statistical estimation method that adds a penalty term to prevent overfitting, mathematically connected to kernel methods and basis expansions.

concepts.name: training-class separation
concepts.description: The degree to which classes can be separated in a feature space, improved by enlarging the space with basis expansions.

concepts.name: kkt conditions
concepts.description: Karush-Kuhn-Tucker optimality conditions that characterize the solution to constrained optimization problems, implying labeled points fall into distinct groups.

concepts.name: feasible point
concepts.description: A point that satisfies all constraints in an optimization problem, used to establish bounds on the objective function.

concepts.name: duality gap
concepts.description: The difference between the primal and dual objective values, which is zero at optimality for convex problems.

concepts.name: linear inequality constraints
concepts.description: Constraints of the form g(x) ≤ 0 in optimization problems, which preserve convexity when combined with a convex objective.

concepts.name: quadratic criterion
concepts.description: An objective function that is quadratic in the optimization variables, ensuring convexity and unique solutions.
